retrieved_docs.title: Intrauterine Device Placement and Removal -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: For the copper IUD, additional contraindications include the following: Wilson disease Sensitivity to copper [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Intrauterine Device Placement and Removal -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Given the 2 classes of IUDs, specific contraindications exist for each type of IUD. However, there are also universal contraindications that are specific to both types. Universal contraindications for the use of IUDs include the following:

retrieved_docs.title: Intrauterine Device Placement and Removal -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The intrauterine device (IUD) is one of the most effective contraception options available today, with failure rates as low as certain sterilization methods. [1] In the United States, 2 types of IUDs currently available—the copper-containing IUD and levonorgestrel-containing IUD, which have similar rates of preventing pregnancy, with failure rates of 0.08% and 0.02%, respectively. These devices are more than 99% effective in preventing pregnancy. [2] Additional benefits of IUDs include efficacy, ease of use, reversible nature, and patient satisfaction, especially for women seeking long-term use and cost-effectiveness. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Intrauterine Device Placement and Removal -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: The first step in setting a patient up for success with an IUD is to provide counseling about the available contraceptive options. Patients should be informed that all forms of LARC, including IUDs and subdermal implants, are highly effective in preventing pregnancy, with efficacy comparable to that of tubal ligation and vasectomy. [8] Strong recommendations support the use of LARC as a first-line option for preventing teenage pregnancies, as IUDs are safe to use in this age group. [9] The Contraceptive CHOICE project has studied the use of LARC and promoted its use by increasing patients' knowledge and acceptability of this form of contraception and removing financial barriers by providing the devices at no cost. [10] By removing these barriers, researchers found that almost two-thirds of the women screened chose LARC options, including both IUDs and subdermal implants. [10] All patients who do not have any contraindications to IUDs should be counseled on the benefits of these devices. Ultimately, it is the patient's choice as to what form of contraception is most beneficial for her.  Once a decision is made, the clinic should facilitate the process by ordering the device and ensuring insurance coverage or obtaining prior authorization for the device and its placement.

retrieved_docs.title: Postcoital Contraception -- Function -- Indications, Contraindications, and Side Effects
retrieved_docs.content: No exams or pregnancy tests are required for providing emergency contraception with oral methods. Bimanual exam and pregnancy tests are needed only prior to inserting Cu IUD. [3] There is reassuring data showing the safety and efficacy of the available EC methods. The side effects are mild, and essentially, no serious adverse events have been reported so far. [10] Since 2013, LNG is made available over the counter (OTC) without age restriction in the United States. In many European countries and Australia, even UPA is available without prescription due to available safety data. [7] Copper IUD requires the patient to schedule an urgent office visit to be inserted within the window and has a risk of perforation (1: 1000), uterine cramping, and heavy menstrual flow in some women. Short-term effects commonly reported with hormonal methods are headaches (19 percent), nausea, and vomiting (12 percent), while drowsiness, spotting, pelvic cramping, and breast tenderness were reported by some patients. [6] No increase in fetal malformation, ectopic pregnancy, or miscarriage was reported with any oral EC methods. Copper IUD is contraindicated in pregnancy, women with menorrhagia, pelvic infection, and Wilson disease. For obese women with body mass index (BMI), more than 30 European guidelines suggest the use of UPA or a higher dose of LNG (3 mg). [2] UPA may not be safe in a patient with uncontrolled asthma requiring oral steroids. Both LNG and UPA may have decreased efficacy among patients on enzyme-inducing drugs (e.g., rifabutin, rifampicin, phenytoin, phenobarbitone, carbamazepine, and St John wort), and Cu-IUD should be considered in those patients. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Intrauterine Device Placement and Removal -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: More common complications associated with IUDs include malpositioned IUDs that do not perforate the myometrium, along with dysmenorrhea and amenorrhea (see Image. Plain Radiograph Showing Intrauterine Device Malposition). [30] Risk factors for malpositioning include obesity, a history of uterine rupture or window, and copper IUD placement. [31] Malpositioned IUDs are often located in the lower uterus, potentially causing abnormal bleeding, pain, and an increased risk of pregnancy. Although not all malpositioned IUDs require removal, removal is generally recommended due to the potential loss of contraceptive efficacy. Reduced efficacy is considered greater with malpositioned copper IUDs than with levonorgestrel-releasing devices.

retrieved_docs.title: Progestins -- Indications -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Individual agents have specific FDA-approved indications. Consult the information for approved indications for particular drugs/formulations in package inserts or other resources. Progestins are used with estrogens for contraceptive purposes: COCs Contraceptive vaginal ring Transdermal patch Progestins are also used without combination with estrogens for contraceptive purposes: POPs: Norethindrone 0.35 mg tablets in the US, desogestrel 75 µg in Europe DMPA injections Etonogestrel subcutaneous implant Levonorgestrel IUD Postcoital contraception: levonorgestrel The other postcoital contraceptive options that do not involve progestins include ulipristal acetate, a progesterone receptor antagonist or modulator, and a copper T 380A IUD.

retrieved_docs.title: Intrauterine Device Placement and Removal -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: After deciding with a patient that an IUD placement is the best contraceptive choice, the procedure is as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Intrauterine Device Placement and Removal -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The intrauterine device is one of the most effective contraceptive methods available, with failure rates comparable to certain forms of sterilization. Intrauterine devices offer numerous advantages, including high efficacy, ease of use, reversibility, and excellent patient satisfaction, particularly for those seeking long-term contraception with cost-effectiveness. There are 2 types of intrauterine devices—copper-containing and levonorgestrel-releasing devices. Although both these devices are indicated for contraception, each type has specific indications based on its unique properties and the specific goals of the patient. Intrauterine devices are primarily placed for nonpregnant women seeking long-term protection against pregnancy. The highest-dose levonorgestrel intrauterine device is also approved for treating menorrhagia and providing endometrial protection during hormone replacement therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Intrauterine Device Placement and Removal -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Once counseling is complete and a negative pregnancy test is confirmed, it is necessary to obtain informed consent. The risks, benefits, and adverse effects of the procedure must be explained to the patient. Risks associated with IUD insertion include pain at the time of insertion; malposition; uterine perforation, which may necessitate surgery; unintended pregnancy; infection; bleeding at the site of insertion; possible expulsion, which may go unnoticed and lead to unintended pregnancy; and alterations in a patient's monthly bleeding pattern. In addition, if pregnancy does occur with an IUD in place, there is a higher risk of ectopic pregnancy or septic abortion. [15] When comparing the copper IUD with the levonorgestrel IUD, there are key differences in the bleeding pattern changes. The copper IUD typically causes menses to become heavier and occasionally longer. [16] Conversely, the levonorgestrel IUD leads to lighter menses and often complete cessation of menstrual bleeding because of the inhibitory action of progesterone on the endometrium. [17] This effect can also be observed with the lower dose levonorgestrel IUDs. However, there is a small percentage of patients who develop irregular bleeding or spotting with lower progesterone levels. [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Intrauterine Device Placement and Removal -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: If these criteria are not met, it is an acceptable practice to bridge the patient with a non-implantable form of contraception, such as oral contraceptives, vaginal rings, transdermal patches, condoms, or medroxyprogesterone acetate injections. [10] If the patient still desires LARC insertion after starting one of these bridging methods, a repeat pregnancy test may be conducted in 3 to 4 weeks. If the result is negative, the patient may undergo LARC placement. [10] For copper IUD placement, the prior method of contraception used does not need to be continued, as the copper IUD is effective immediately. However, if the levonorgestrel IUD is not placed within 7 days of the start of menses, an additional form of contraception should be used for 7 days. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Postcoital Contraception -- Function -- Methods Available
retrieved_docs.content: Based on the available literature, EC has been in use since 1960 and originally consisted of a high dose of oral estrogen, which soon was phased out due to increased side effects. [7] Copper intrauterine devices (IUD) and Yuzpe methods became popular in the 1970s. The first dedicated U.S Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approval for the product for EC was in 1998. [6] Several products have been introduced since that time. The oral hormonal method and the non-hormonal copper intrauterine device (Cu-IUD) are the two available groups for emergency contraception for women to avoid unintended pregnancy. Levonorgestrel (LNG), ulipristal acetate (UPA), and Yuzpe method using combined estrogen-progestin pills are the 3 currently approved oral hormonal methods in the United States. Mifepristone, which acts by inhibiting progesterone, is available for use as an emergency contraceptive in other countries. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Postcoital Contraception -- Function -- Indications, Contraindications, and Side Effects
retrieved_docs.content: As per the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecology (ACOG) Practice Bulletin on EC, both oral hormonal ECs using UPA, combined estrogen and progestin pills, and Cu IUD should be made available to women for up to 5 days after UPSI when requested. To increase the effectiveness, treatment should be initiated as soon as possible. LNG has decreasing efficacy with time and is labeled for use within 72 hours. Cu-IUD and ulipristal acetate do not have a change in efficacy with time and should be considered as the first line if the patient presents late after UPSI. Some studies have used Cu-IUD up to 10 days after UPSI without any failure. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Intrauterine Device Placement and Removal -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy or suspected pregnancy Sexually transmitted infection at the time of IUD insertion, including cervicitis, vaginitis, or any other lower genital tract infection Congenital uterine abnormality that distorts the shape of the uterine cavity, making insertion difficult Acute pelvic inflammatory disease History of pelvic inflammatory disease, unless a subsequent successful intrauterine pregnancy has occurred History of septic abortion or history of postpartum endometritis within the last 3 months Confirmed or suspicion of uterine or cervical malignancy or neoplasia Abnormal uterine bleeding of unknown origin Any condition that increases the risk of pelvic infection History of previously inserted IUD that has not been removed Hypersensitivity to any component of the device [6] Specific levonorgestrel-releasing IUD contraindications are as follows: Confirmed or suspicion of breast malignancy or other progestin-sensitive cancer Liver tumors, benign or malignant Acute liver disease [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Intrauterine Device Placement and Removal -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Once uterine depth is determined, follow manufacturer instructions for inserting the specific IUD.

Levonorgestrel, however, is contraindicated in individuals who have a uterine lining tumor (ورم بطانة الرحم), indicating that this drug should be avoided in patients with such a condition.

retrieved_docs.content: pharmacologic class, mechanism of action, and therapeutic context of each agent, particularly when selecting combination hormone products like ESTROGENS/MEDROXYPROGESTERONE for hormone replacement or contraceptive purposes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 196
retrieved_docs.content: levonorgestrel and medroxyprogesterone acetate represent key progestogenic components of combined hormonal contraceptives, both acting on the progesterone receptor, while leuprolide provides a therapeutic option for uterine lining tumors that are contraindicated for levonorgestrel use. Polyethylene glycol 400 functions as a common inactive excipient in the oral formulations containing these active agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 682
retrieved_docs.content: Dienogest is a progestogenic component of the combined oral contraceptive formulation dienogest/estradiol, and this combination product is classified within the group of progestogens and estrogens systemic contraceptives, sequential preparations. The use of dienogest, either alone or as part of the dienogest/estradiol preparation, is contraindicated in patients with مرض الشريان التاجي (coronary artery disease), reflecting the need to avoid this hormone therapy in individuals with established coronary vascular pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 63
retrieved_docs.content: Levonorgestrel is a synthetic progestin that belongs to the class of hormonal contraceptive agents and is included in the broader category of female contraceptive drugs. It is a key active ingredient in several combined oral tablets that also contain ethinyl estradiol, such as the 0.02 mg/0.1 mg, 0.03 mg/0.05 mg, and 0.03 mg/0.15 mg formulations. These tablets use polyethylene glycol 400 as an inactive excipient. Human progesterone receptors are a pharmacologic target of levonorgestrel, mediating its contraceptive effects. Because levonorgestrel can stimulate progesterone‑responsive tissues, it is contraindicated in patients with uterine (endometrial) cancer. Leuprolide, another compound listed under the same “L [Preparations]” grouping, is a gonadotropin‑releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist that may be employed in the treatment of uterine cancer, highlighting a therapeutic role that contrasts with the contraindication of levonorgestrel in the same disease. Medroxyprogesterone acetate is also classified as a hormonal and female contraceptive agent. Like levonorgestrel, it acts on the human progesterone receptor, providing progestogenic activity for contraception and other hormonal indications. Together, these relationships illustrate how levonorgestrel and medroxyprogesterone acetate function as progesterone‑receptor‑targeting contraceptives, how levonorgestrel is formulated in specific oral tablets with ethinyl estradiol and polyethylene glycol 400, and how the safety profile of levonorgestrel requires avoidance in uterine cancer, whereas leuprolide may be used therapeutically for that condition. This integrated knowledge supports informed prescribing, patient counseling, and risk management in reproductive and oncologic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: progestogen‑estrogen fixed‑dose combinations is defined as formulations that contain both a progestogen and an estrogen in a single tablet. The specific ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel tablets (0.02 mg/0.1 mg, 0.03 mg/0.05 mg, 0.03 mg/0.15 mg) uniformly list polyethylene glycol 400 as an inactive ingredient, indicating its role in tablet manufacture and stability across dosage strengths. Together, levonorgestrel and medroxyprogesterone acetate illustrate the interconnected nature of synthetic progestins: both act on the progesterone receptor, are integral components of combined hormonal contraceptives and hormone‑therapy regimens, possess defined parent‑child relationships within pharmaceutical preparations, and have specific contraindications or therapeutic applications in endometrial pathology, underscoring their significance in reproductive health and disease management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 148
retrieved_docs.content: how levonorgestrel and medroxyprogesterone acetate are integral components of hormonal contraceptive regimens, how their molecular targets (progesterone receptor) and specific contraindications (uterine cancer) guide safe patient use, and how they coexist with other therapeutic agents (leuprolide, loperamide, methyltestosterone) and excipients (polyethylene glycol 400, powdered cellulose) within broader pharmaceutical preparations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 0 -- Community 12
retrieved_docs.content: with other agents for specific therapeutic goals. Levonorgestrel, derived from norgestrel, is the active moiety of several combined oral contraceptives (ethinyl estradiol 0.02 mg/levonorgestrel 0.1 mg; ethinyl estradiol 0.03 mg/levonorgestrel 0.05 mg; ethinyl estradiol 0.03 mg/levonorgestrel 0.15 mg) and of formulations that also contain folic acid. It is categorized as a hormonal contraceptive and as an emergency‑contraception agent, acts on the human progesterone receptor, and is contraindicated in patients with endometrial carcinoma. Medroxyprogesterone acetate is the active ingredient of the combination product ESTROGENS/MEDROXYPROGESTERONE and is formulated with conjugated estrogens in fixed‑dose oral packs such as Prempro 0.625/2.5 mg and Prempro 0.625/5 mg (28‑day regimens). These preparations belong to the class of progestogen‑and‑estrogen fixed combinations used for menopausal symptom management; the acetate ester is classified under female contraceptive agents and hormonal

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 498
retrieved_docs.content: L [Preparations] is a product family that includes both levonorgestrel and leuprolid. Levonorgestrel serves as the active hormonal component in several combined oral contraceptive tablets—ethinyl estradiol 0.02 mg/levonorgestrel 0.1 mg, ethinyl estradiol 0.03 mg/levonorgestrel 0.05 mg, and ethinyl estradiol 0.03 mg/levonorgestrel 0.15 mg—and is also present in a formulation that combines ethinyl estradiol, folic acid, and levonorgestrel. All of these tablets share polyethylene glycol 400 as an inactive ingredient. While levonorgestrel is contraindicated in patients with a uterine cavity tumor (ورم بطانة الرحم), leuprolid is employed in the therapeutic management of such uterine tumors. This contrast underscores the importance of selecting the appropriate agent based on the underlying gynecologic condition and highlights the safety considerations that must be observed when prescribing hormonal preparations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 248
retrieved_docs.content: drugs. Sequential systemic contraceptive preparations, including emergency contraception, are grouped under hormonal contraceptives for systemic use. This hierarchical framework links agents by chemical structure, therapeutic indication (contraception, endocrine therapy, receptor modulation), and formulation type, providing a coherent reference for prescribing, research, and pharmacological categorization.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2563
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes mellitus is clinically associated with severe foot complications, including the need for toe amputation, reflecting the disease’s impact on peripheral vascular health and neuropathy. In addition, the emergency contraceptive norgestrel is contraindicated for patients with diabetes, indicating that clinicians should avoid prescribing norgestrel to individuals with this condition due to potential safety concerns. Recognizing both the risk of toe amputation and the contraindication of norgestrel underscores the importance of careful management and medication selection in diabetic patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
retrieved_docs.content: various progestogen and estrogen derivatives, their placement within ATC categories, and their therapeutic roles in systemic hormonal contraception, endocrine therapy, and combined hormonal regimens, thereby informing clinical pharmacology and patient‑care decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1369
retrieved_docs.content: Medroxyprogesterone and megestrol are both classified as progestogen systemic hormonal contraceptives, meaning they are progestogen‑based agents administered systemically for contraceptive purposes. As members of this drug class, they share the common mechanism of providing hormonal contraception through progestogenic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2369
retrieved_docs.content: The oral tablet labeled “ethinyl estradiol 0.03 MG / levonorgestrel 0.05 MG Oral Tablet” includes levonorgestrel as an active ingredient, and the combination product “ethinyl estradiol/folic acid/levonorgestrel” also contains levonorgestrel. Levonorgestrel is a synthetic progestin that is chemically derived from its parent compound norgestrel. Recognizing that both formulations deliver levonorgestrel and that levonorgestrel originates from norgestrel helps clinicians understand the pharmacologic basis, potential cross‑reactivity, and therapeutic expectations when prescribing these hormonal contraceptive products.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 28
retrieved_docs.content: Levonorgestrel is a synthetic progestogen that serves as the active ingredient in several combined oral contraceptive tablets with ethinyl estradiol (ethinyl estradiol 0.02 mg/levonorgestrel 0.1 mg, ethinyl estradiol 0.03 mg/levonorgestrel 0.05 mg, and ethinyl estradiol 0.03 mg/levonorgestrel 0.15 mg). In these formulations, polyethylene glycol 400 is used as an inactive excipient that functions as a filler or solvent, influencing tablet physical properties without pharmacologic activity and potentially affecting patients with excipient sensitivities. Levonorgestrel binds the human progesterone receptor, a molecular target it shares with the progestin medroxyprogesterone acetate. Medroxyprogesterone acetate is also classified as a hormonal contraceptive agent and appears in fixed‑dose combination hormone products marketed as Prempro (ESTROGENS/MEDROXYPROGESTERONE), which contain conjugated estrogens 0.625 mg combined with either 2.5 mg or 5 mg of medroxyprogesterone acetate. These combination products belong to the broader class of progestogens and estrogens in combination therapy, and they likewise list polyethylene glycol 400 as an inactive ingredient. Leuprolide, a member of the L [Preparations] family, is indicated for the treatment of uterine lining tumors. In contrast, levonorgestrel is contraindicated in patients with uterine lining tumors, highlighting a therapeutic overlap in the management of this condition. Overall, the knowledge base links synthetic progestins such as levonorgestrel and medroxyprogesterone acetate to hormonal contraception, identifies the progesterone receptor as their shared pharmacologic target, details the active and inactive components of their formulations, and outlines specific clinical considerations, including contraindications related to uterine lining tumors.
